comment_text.sn10.5:1.1: The commentary explains that Sānu was a disillusioned novice who had returned home planning to disrobe. | Sānu was perhaps named after the planet Saturn (Sanskrit śani; cf. SN 10.7). The Sanskrit word for planet (graha, cf. Pali gayha at DN 20:17.3) shows their role as “seizers”. The later Suśrutasaṁhitā-uttaratantra details the entities that cause such seizures in children (60), corresponding to the nine planets (27). Seizures or fits were treated as medical, spiritual, and astrological in cause and remedy.
comment_text.sn10.5:1.2: Elsewhere we find possession by Māra expressed as anvāviṭṭha, literally “inhabited”, where the victims are made to behave badly but are otherwise normal (SN 4.18:1.4, MN 50:14.2; cf. Sanskrit āviṣṭa). Gahita here is also used for Rāhu “seizing” the Sun and Moon (SN 2.10:1.2). The commentary says he was shaking on the ground, eyes rolling and mouth foaming, i.e. having a “seizure”. This was not ill-motivated, for the yakkha was trying to prevent him from disrobing. Compare those who, though “seized by a gandharva” (gandharvagṛhīta), remain perfectly cogent (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.3.1, 3.7.1).
comment_text.sn10.5:1.3: PTS editions insert an extra verse below, but it seems to be a scribal error.
comment_text.sn10.5:2.1: See AN 3.70 for a detailed description of the eight-factored sabbath.
comment_text.sn10.5:6.2: It’s interesting that the Buddha cites a yakkha saying to someone recovering from a yakkha-induced seizure. At Thig 12.1:12.1 these lines are attributed to the nun Puṇṇikā.
comment_text.sn10.5:8.1: Sānu has recovered from his seizure to see his weeping mother. This verse appears without any context at Thag 1.44.
comment_text.sn10.5:9.1: Here’s the plot twist: the mother’s real worry is not the seizure, but that her son will fall prey to desire and suffering. See SN 20.10:3.5, “For it is death in the training of the Noble One to reject the training and return to a lesser life.”
comment_text.sn10.5:10.3: This is, I think, the only case in the EBTs where “inferno” might be suggested for naraka. Elsewhere in early Pali, however, it means “cliff, abyss” (MN 49:5.9, mn86:6.15](), Snp 3.11:28.4, Thag 16.8:4.2).
comment_text.sn10.5:11.1: Here we see the origin of the term of address bhante, i.e. “good luck to thee”.

root_text.sn10.5:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn10.5:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena aññatarissā upāsikāya sānu nāma putto yakkhena gahito hoti.
root_text.sn10.5:1.3: Atha kho sā upāsikā paridevamānā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imā gāthāyo abhāsi:

translation_text.sn10.5:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn10.5:1.2: Now at that time a certain lay woman had a son named Sānu who had been seized by a native spirit.
translation_text.sn10.5:1.3: And as that lay woman wept, on that occasion she recited these verses:
translation_text.sn10.5:2.1: “I have heard this from the perfected ones.
translation_text.sn10.5:2.2: The native spirits will not mess with anyone
translation_text.sn10.5:3.2: on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days,
translation_text.sn10.5:3.3: and the eighth day of the fortnight,
translation_text.sn10.5:3.4: as well as on the fortnight of special displays.
translation_text.sn10.5:4.1: “What you heard from the perfected ones is right.
translation_text.sn10.5:4.2: The native spirits will not mess with anyone
translation_text.sn10.5:5.2: on the fourteenth and the fifteenth days,
translation_text.sn10.5:5.3: and the eighth day of the fortnight,
translation_text.sn10.5:5.4: as well as on the fortnight of special displays.
translation_text.sn10.5:6.1: When Sānu regains consciousness tell him
translation_text.sn10.5:6.2: this saying of the native spirits:
translation_text.sn10.5:7.1: If you should do a bad deed,
translation_text.sn10.5:7.3: you won’t be freed from suffering,
translation_text.sn10.5:7.4: though you fly away and flee.”
translation_text.sn10.5:8.2: or for one who’s alive but missing.
translation_text.sn10.5:8.3: I’m alive and you can see me,
translation_text.sn10.5:8.4: so mum, why do you weep for me?”
translation_text.sn10.5:9.2: or for one who’s alive but missing.
translation_text.sn10.5:9.3: But someone who has given up sensual pleasures
translation_text.sn10.5:9.4: only to come back here again:
translation_text.sn10.5:9.5: they cry for them as well,
translation_text.sn10.5:9.6: for though still alive they’re really dead.
translation_text.sn10.5:10.1: My dear, you’ve been rescued from hot coals,
translation_text.sn10.5:10.2: and you want to plunge right back in them!
translation_text.sn10.5:10.3: My dear, you’ve been rescued from the abyss,
translation_text.sn10.5:10.4: and you want to plunge right back there!
translation_text.sn10.5:11.1: Hurry up, good luck to you!
translation_text.sn10.5:11.3: A valuable saved from a fire,

variant_text.sn10.5:7.1: Sace ca → yañceva (bj); saceva (sya-all, km, pts1ed, mr)
variant_text.sn10.5:11.3: nīhataṁ → nīhaṭaṁ (bj); nibbhataṁ (sya-all, km, mr); nibhataṁ (pts1ed, mr)
